== In literature ==
In her poem Calypso Watching the Ocean, Letitia Landon describes her as eternally yearning for Odysseus' return and comments on the folly of such obsession.


== Philosophy ==
Philosophers have written about the meaning of Calypso in the world of ancient Greece. Ryan Patrick Hanley commented on the interpretation of Calypso in Les Aventures de Télémaque written by Fénelon. Hanley says that the story of Calypso illustrates the link between Eros and pride. Title: Calypso (mythology)

Source: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calypso_(mythology)

In Greek mythology, Calypso (; Ancient Greek: Καλυψώ, romanized: Kalypsṓ, lit. 'she who conceals') was a nymph who lived on the island of Ogygia, where, according to Homer's Odyssey, she detained Odysseus for seven years against his will. She promised Odysseus immortality if he would stay with her, but Odysseus preferred to return home. Eventually, after the intervention of the other gods, Calypso was forced to let Odysseus go.


== Etymology ==
The name Calypso derives from the Ancient Greek καλύπτω (kalyptō), meaning 'to cover', 'to conceal', or 'to hide'; as such, her name translates to 'she who conceals' as she conceals Odysseus from the rest of the world, keeping him on her island.
According to the medieval dictionary Etymologicum Magnum, her name means 'concealing the knowledge' (from Greek: καλύπτουσα το διανοούμενον, romanized: kalýptousa to dianooúmenon), which – combined with the Homeric epithet δολόεσσα (dolóessa, meaning 'subtle' or 'wily') – justifies the reclusive character of Calypso and her island.


== Family ==
Calypso is generally said to be the daughter of the Titan Atlas. In the Fabulae, she is born to Pleione, the mother of the Pleiades, though this is the only source in which this parentage appears.
Hesiod and the anonymous author of the Homeric Hymn to Demeter mention either a different Calypso or possibly the same Calypso as one of the Oceanid nymphs, daughters of Tethys and Oceanus. Apollodorus includes the name Calypso in his list of Nereids, the daughters of Nereus and Doris. John Tzetzes meanwhile makes her a daughter of the sun-god Helios and the Oceanid nymph Perse, who are also the parents of Circe, perhaps due to her association with Circe; the two goddesses were sometimes confused due to their behaviour and connection to Odysseus.
According to a fragment from the Catalogue of Women, Calypso bore the Cephalonians to Hermes, as suggested by Hermes' visits to her island in the Odyssey.


== Mythology ==


=== The Odyssey ===
In Homer's Odyssey, Calypso tries to keep the fabled Greek hero Odysseus on her island to make him her immortal husband, while he also gets to enjoy her sensual pleasures forever. According to Homer, Calypso kept Odysseus prisoner by force at Ogygia for seven years. Calypso enchants Odysseus with her singing as she moves to and from, weaving on her loom with a golden shuttle.
Odysseus comes to wish for circumstances to change. He can no longer bear being separated from his wife, Penelope, and wants to tell Calypso. He spends the daytime sitting on a headland or at the sea-shore crying, while at night he is forced to sleep with her in the cave against his will. His patron goddess Athena asks Zeus to order the release of Odysseus from the island; Zeus orders the messenger Hermes to tell Calypso to set Odysseus free, for it was not Odysseus's destiny to live with her forever. She angrily comments on how the gods hate goddesses having affairs with mortals.
Calypso provides Odysseus with an axe, drill, and adze to build a boat. Calypso leads Odysseus to an island where he can chop down trees and make planks for his boat. Calypso also provides him with wine, bread, clothing, and more materials for his boat. The goddess then sets wind at his back when he sets sail. After seven years, Odysseus has built his boat and leaves Calypso.


=== Other narratives ===
Homer does not mention any children by Calypso. By some accounts that came after the Odyssey, Calypso bore Odysseus a son, Latinus, though Circe is usually given as Latinus' mother. In other accounts, Calypso bore Odysseus two children, Nausithous and Nausinous. 
The story of Odysseus and Calypso has some close resemblances to the interactions between Gilgamesh and Siduri in the Epic of Gilgamesh in that "the lone female plies the inconsolable hero-wanderer with drink and sends him off to a place beyond the sea reserved for a special class of honoured people" and "to prepare for the voyage he has to cut down and trim timbers".
A fragment from the Catalogue of Women, erroneously attributed to Hesiod, claimed that Calypso detained Odysseus for years as a favour to Poseidon, the sea-god who detested Odysseus for blinding his son, the cyclops Polyphemus.
According to Hyginus, Calypso killed herself because of her love for Odysseus.


== In literature ==
In her poem Calypso Watching the Ocean, Letitia Landon describes her as eternally yearning for Odysseus' return and comments on the folly of such obsession.


== Philosophy ==
Philosophers have written about the meaning of Calypso in the world of ancient Greece. Ryan Patrick Hanley commented on the interpretation of Calypso in Les Aventures de Télé

Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) must be kept in their original language and not translated.\n\n---Data to be Processed---\n<Entity_types>\n[Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject]\n\n<Input Text>\n```\nTitle: Calypso (mythology)\n\nSource: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calypso_(mythology)\n\nIn Greek mythology, Calypso (; Ancient Greek: Καλυψώ, romanized: Kalypsṓ, lit. 'she who conceals') was a nymph who lived on the island of Ogygia, where, according to Homer's Odyssey, she detained Odysseus for seven years against his will. She promised Odysseus immortality if he would stay with her, but Odysseus preferred to return home. Eventually, after the intervention of the other gods, Calypso was forced to let Odysseus go.\n\n\n== Etymology ==\nThe name Calypso derives from the Ancient Greek καλύπτω (kalyptō), meaning 'to cover', 'to conceal', or 'to hide'; as such, her name translates to 'she who conceals' as she conceals Odysseus from the rest of the world, keeping him on her island.\nAccording to the medieval dictionary Etymologicum Magnum, her name means 'concealing the knowledge' (from Greek: καλύπτουσα το διανοούμενον, romanized: kalýptousa to dianooúmenon), which – combined with the Homeric epithet δολόεσσα (dolóessa, meaning 'subtle' or 'wily') – justifies the reclusive character of Calypso and her island.\n\n\n== Family ==\nCalypso is generally said to be the daughter of the Titan Atlas. In the Fabulae, she is born to Pleione, the mother of the Pleiades, though this is the only source in which this parentage appears.\nHesiod and the anonymous author of the Homeric Hymn to Demeter mention either a different Calypso or possibly the same Calypso as one of the Oceanid nymphs, daughters of Tethys and Oceanus. Apollodorus includes the name Calypso in his list of Nereids, the daughters of Nereus and Doris. John Tzetzes meanwhile makes her a daughter of the sun-god Helios and the Oceanid nymph Perse, who are also the parents of Circe, perhaps due to her association with Circe; the two goddesses were sometimes confused due to their behaviour and connection to Odysseus.\nAccording to a fragment from the Catalogue of Women, Calypso bore the Cephalonians to Hermes, as suggested by Hermes' visits to her island in the Odyssey.\n\n\n== Mythology ==\n\n\n=== The Odyssey ===\nIn Homer's Odyssey, Calypso tries to keep the fabled Greek hero Odysseus on her island to make him her immortal husband, while he also gets to enjoy her sensual pleasures forever. According to Homer, Calypso kept Odysseus prisoner by force at Ogygia for seven years. Calypso enchants Odysseus with her singing as she moves to and from, weaving on her loom with a golden shuttle.\nOdysseus comes to wish for circumstances to change. He can no longer bear being separated from his wife, Penelope, and wants to tell Calypso. He spends the daytime sitting on a headland or at the sea-shore crying, while at night he is forced to sleep with her in the cave against his will. His patron goddess Athena asks Zeus to order the release of Odysseus from the island; Zeus orders the messenger Hermes to tell Calypso to set Odysseus free, for it was not Odysseus's destiny to live with her forever. She angrily comments on how the gods hate goddesses having affairs with mortals.\nCalypso provides Odysseus with an axe, drill, and adze to build a boat. Calypso leads Odysseus to an island where he can chop down trees and make planks for his boat. Calypso also provides him with wine, bread, clothing, and more materials for his boat. The goddess then sets wind at his back when he sets sail. After seven years, Odysseus has built his boat and leaves Calypso.\n\n\n=== Other narratives ===\nHomer does not mention any children by Calypso. ---Role---
You are a Knowledge Graph Specialist responsible for extracting entities and relationships from the input text.

---Instructions---
1.  **Entity Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify clearly defined and meaningful entities in the input text.
    *   **Entity Details:** For each identified entity, extract the following information:
        *   `entity_name`: The name of the entity. If the entity name is case-insensitive, capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case). Ensure **consistent naming** across the entire extraction process.
        *   `entity_type`: Categorize the entity using one of the following types: `Person,Creature,Organization,Location,Event,Concept,Method,Content,Data,Artifact,NaturalObject`. If none of the provided entity types apply, do not add new entity type and classify it as `Other`.
        *   `entity_description`: Provide a concise yet comprehensive description of the entity's attributes and activities, based *solely* on the information present in the input text.
    *   **Output Format - Entities:** Output a total of 4 fields for each entity, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `entity`.
        *   Format: `entity<|#|>entity_name<|#|>entity_type<|#|>entity_description`

2.  **Relationship Extraction & Output:**
    *   **Identification:** Identify direct, clearly stated, and meaningful relationships between previously extracted entities.
    *   **N-ary Relationship Decomposition:** If a single statement describes a relationship involving more than two entities (an N-ary relationship), decompose it into multiple binary (two-entity) relationship pairs for separate description.
        *   **Example:** For "Alice, Bob, and Carol collaborated on Project X," extract binary relationships such as "Alice collaborated with Project X," "Bob collaborated with Project X," and "Carol collaborated with Project X," or "Alice collaborated with Bob," based on the most reasonable binary interpretations.
    *   **Relationship Details:** For each binary relationship, extract the following fields:
        *   `source_entity`: The name of the source entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `target_entity`: The name of the target entity. Ensure **consistent naming** with entity extraction. Capitalize the first letter of each significant word (title case) if the name is case-insensitive.
        *   `relationship_keywords`: One or more high-level keywords summarizing the overarching nature, concepts, or themes of the relationship. Multiple keywords within this field must be separated by a comma `,`. **DO NOT use `<|#|>` for separating multiple keywords within this field.**
        *   `relationship_description`: A concise explanation of the nature of the relationship between the source and target entities, providing a clear rationale for their connection.
    *   **Output Format - Relationships:** Output a total of 5 fields for each relationship, delimited by `<|#|>`, on a single line. The first field *must* be the literal string `relation`.
        *   Format: `relation<|#|>source_entity<|#|>target_entity<|#|>relationship_keywords<|#|>relationship_description`

3.  **Delimiter Usage Protocol:**
    *   The `<|#|>` is a complete, atomic marker and **must not be filled with content**. It serves strictly as a field separator.
    *   **Incorrect Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|location|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`
    *   **Correct Example:** `entity<|#|>Tokyo<|#|>location<|#|>Tokyo is the capital of Japan.`

4.  **Relationship Direction & Duplication:**
    *   Treat all relationships as **undirected** unless explicitly stated otherwise. Swapping the source and target entities for an undirected relationship does not constitute a new relationship.
    *   Avoid outputting duplicate relationships.

5.  **Output Order & Prioritization:**
    *   Output all extracted entities first, followed by all extracted relationships.
    *   Within the list of relationships, prioritize and output those relationships that are **most significant** to the core meaning of the input text first.

6.  **Context & Objectivity:**
    *   Ensure all entity names and descriptions are written in the **third person**.
    *   Explicitly name the subject or object; **avoid using pronouns** such as `this article`, `this paper`, `our company`, `I`, `you`, and `he/she`.

7.  **Language & Proper Nouns:**
    *   The entire output (entity names, keywords, and descriptions) must be written in `English`.
    *   Proper nouns (e.g., personal names, place names, organization names) should be retained in their original language if a proper, widely accepted translation is not available or would cause ambiguity.

**Greek mythology and Calypso**

Within Greek mythology, Calypso is a notable figure connected especially to the story of Odysseus in Homer's *Odyssey*:

- **Who Calypso is:**  
  Calypso is a nymph who lives on the remote island of **Ogygia**. Her name comes from the Greek verb *kalyptō* (“to cover, conceal, hide”), and is explained as “she who conceals,” since she hides Odysseus from the rest of the world on her island.[1]

- **Role in the Odyssey:**  
  According to the *Odyssey*, Calypso detains the hero **Odysseus** on Ogygia for **seven years** against his will. She offers him **immortality** if he will stay with her as her husband, but he longs instead to return home to his wife **Penelope**.[1]

- **Divine intervention:**  
  Odysseus’s patron goddess **Athena** asks **Zeus** to free him. Zeus then orders **Hermes** to tell Calypso to release Odysseus, declaring it is not his destiny to live with her forever. Though angry at the gods’ double standards about goddesses and mortal lovers, Calypso obeys.[1]

- **Odysseus’s departure:**  
  Calypso helps him leave: she gives him an axe, drill, and adze, leads him to trees for timber, and supplies **wine, bread, clothing**, and other materials for his **boat**, even sending a favorable wind when he sails away.[1]

- **Family and varying traditions:**  
  In Greek mythic genealogies, Calypso’s parentage varies:
  - Commonly a **daughter of the Titan Atlas**  
  - In other sources, one of the **Oceanid nymphs** (daughters of **Tethys** and **Oceanus**), or one of the **Nereids** (daughters of **Nereus** and **Doris**)  
  - Another tradition makes her a daughter of **Helios** and the Oceanid **Perse**, also the parents of **Circe**, with whom she is sometimes confused because both detain Odysseus.[1]

- **Later stories:**  
  Later myths sometimes say she bore Odysseus children (such as **Latinus**, or two sons **Nausithous** and **Nausinous**), and one tradition has her bearing the **Cephalonians** to **Hermes**. Another fragment claims she kept Odysseus as a favor to **Poseidon**, who hated Odysseus for blinding his cyclops son **Polyphemus**. In **Hyginus**, Calypso ultimately **kills herself** because of her love for Odysseus.[1][2]

- **Interpretations and philosophy:**  
  Philosophers and later writers have reflected on what Calypso represents within ancient Greek culture. Her story has been used to discuss themes such as the link between **Eros (love/desire)** and **pride**, and the way her depiction combines **power over fate** with a domestic, “bourgeois housewife” sensibility. Literary works like Letitia Landon’s poem *Calypso Watching the Ocean* further portray her as eternally yearning for Odysseus and highlight the **folly of obsessive love**.[2][3]

Calypso is a nymph from Greek mythology, best known from Homer's *Odyssey*.

- **Nature and name**: She is a divine nymph whose name comes from the Greek verb *kalyptō* (“to cover, conceal, hide”). Her name thus means **“she who conceals”**, reflecting how she keeps Odysseus hidden from the world on her remote island. A medieval source also glosses her name as **“concealing the knowledge,”** and she is given the Homeric epithet **dolóessa** (“subtle, wily”), which fits her reclusive, mysterious character and isolated island home.[1]

- **Home**: Calypso lives on the island of **Ogygia**, a distant, secluded place in the sea.[1]

- **Role in the *Odyssey***:  
  - She **detains the hero Odysseus for seven years** on Ogygia, keeping him there against his will.  
  - She offers him **immortality** if he will stay with her and become her husband, but he longs instead to return to his wife **Penelope** and his home.[1]  
  - Odysseus spends his days on the shore weeping for home, while at night he is compelled to share Calypso’s bed in her cave.[1]  
  - The goddess **Athena**, Odysseus’ protector, persuades **Zeus** to order his release. Zeus sends **Hermes** to Calypso with the command that Odysseus must go, because it is **not his destiny to live with her forever**.[1]  
  - Though angry at the gods’ interference (and complaining about how goddesses are punished for loving mortal men), Calypso obeys: she **helps Odysseus build a boat**, giving him an axe, drill, adze, and other materials, and **supplies wine, bread, clothing**, and favorable wind for his departure.[1]

- **Family (varying traditions)**:  
  Ancient sources disagree about her parentage:  
  - Most commonly, she is said to be the **daughter of the Titan Atlas**.[1]  
  - One source (*Fabulae*) calls her a daughter of **Pleione**.[1]  
  - Hesiod and the *Homeric Hymn to Demeter* mention a Calypso among the **Oceanid nymphs**, daughters of **Tethys and Oceanus**.[1]  
  - Apollodorus lists a Calypso among the **Nereids**, daughters of **Nereus and Doris**.[1]  
  - John Tzetzes makes her a daughter of **Helios** and the Oceanid **Perse**, which would make her a sister of **Circe**; the two are sometimes confused because of their similar behavior toward Odysseus.[1]

- **Later stories and interpretations**:  
  - Homer does **not** give her children, but later accounts say she bore Odysseus a son **Latinus**, or two sons **Nausithous** and **Nausinous**.[1]  
  - A fragment called the *Catalogue of Women* claims she bore the **Cephalonians** to **Hermes**.[1]  
  - Another fragment says she kept Odysseus as a **favor to Poseidon**, who hated Odysseus for blinding his Cyclops son **Polyphemus**.[1]  
  - According to **Hyginus**, Calypso **killed herself** because of her love for Odysseus.[1]  
  - In Letitia Landon’s poem **“Calypso Watching the Ocean,”** she is portrayed as **eternally yearning** for Odysseus’ return, and the poem comments on the **folly of such obsession**.[1]  
  - Philosophers have used her story to discuss the **meaning of Calypso** in ancient Greek culture; Ryan Patrick Hanley links her story to the relation between **Eros and pride**, and Theodor Adorno and Max Horkheimer highlight how her depiction combines **“power over fate”** with a **“bourgeois housewives” sensibility**.[1]

In summary, Calypso is the immortal nymph who loves Odysseus, hides him on her island for seven years, offers him immortality, and ultimately must let him go when the gods decree that his fate lies elsewhere.

Calypso’s fate depends on which source you look at.

### In Homer’s Odyssey  
In the Odyssey, we are simply told that:

- Calypso is a nymph who lives alone on the island of Ogygia and detains Odysseus there for seven years against his will.  
- She promises him immortality if he will stay and be her husband, but he prefers to return home.  
- After Athena appeals to Zeus, the gods order Calypso to release Odysseus. Hermes delivers the command.  
- Though angry at the double standard about goddesses and mortal lovers, she obeys: she helps Odysseus build a boat, provides him with tools, timber, food (bread and wine), clothing, and other materials, and sends him off with a favorable wind.  
- Homer does not say that she dies or otherwise specify what happens to her afterward—she remains on Ogygia once Odysseus departs. [1]

So in the original epic, “what happens to Calypso” is left open; the story follows Odysseus, not her.

### Later Mythographers (Hyginus)  
Later authors try to fill this gap. The mythographer Hyginus reports a much more tragic ending:

- According to Hyginus, Calypso killed herself because of her love for Odysseus. [1]

In this later tradition, her unrequited love and the loss of Odysseus lead to her suicide, giving a definitive and sorrowful end to her story.

### Later Literary Interpretations  
Later literature sometimes imagines her as endlessly stuck in longing:

- Letitia Landon’s poem *Calypso Watching the Ocean* portrays Calypso as eternally yearning for Odysseus’ return and explicitly comments on the “folly” of such obsessive desire. [1]

This doesn’t claim to be historical myth, but it shows how her fate has been reimagined as one of endless, unresolved longing rather than closure.

---

In sum: Homer leaves Calypso alive on Ogygia after Odysseus sails away; a later source (Hyginus) says she kills herself for love; and later literature often treats her as a figure of permanent, obsessive yearning.

Calypso’s relationship to Odysseus is defined by her intense love for him and her long, forced detention of him on her island:

- **Captor and lover:** Calypso is a nymph who lives on the island of Ogygia. According to *Homer’s Odyssey*, she **detains Odysseus there for seven years against his will**, trying to keep him as her husband and enchanting him with her singing and sensual pleasures. She promises to make him **immortal if he stays with her**, but he wishes to return home instead.[1]

- **Conflict of desires:** While Calypso loves Odysseus and wants him to remain with her, Odysseus spends his days weeping on the shore, longing for his wife **Penelope** and his home. He is compelled to sleep with Calypso at night but emotionally remains attached to his life in Ithaca.[1]

- **Divine intervention and release:** Odysseus’s patron goddess **Athena** asks **Zeus** to free him; Zeus sends **Hermes** to order Calypso to let Odysseus go, declaring it is not his destiny to stay with her forever. Calypso obeys, though angrily, and then **helps him build and provision a boat**, giving him tools, food, clothing, and favorable wind so he can depart.[1]

- **Possible children (later traditions):** Homer does *not* mention children from their union. Later accounts vary: some say Calypso bore Odysseus **a son Latinus**, while others say she had **two sons, Nausithous and Nausinous**, by him.[1]

- **Aftermath in later myth:** In one later source, **Hyginus** reports that Calypso **killed herself because of her love for Odysseus**, emphasizing how one‑sided and consuming her attachment was.[1]

In sum, Calypso is Odysseus’s immortal lover and captor, offering him eternal life and desiring him deeply, while he longs only to leave her island and return to his mortal wife and home.
